reasoning: All four correctly diagnose the buried retail premise and converge on the same option family (vacancy intervention, residential/anchor demand, experiential clustering), so discrimination comes from rigor of experimental design, calibration, and originality of mechanism. B wins on rigor: it attaches an explicit falsifier and a single shared metric (presence-hours per block) to every option and engineers the pilot itself to separate the two competing hypotheses (regulatory rigidity vs. genuinely absent population/transit), while naming Goodhart and the systems leverage hierarchy. A is a very close second with the best calibration in the set (explicit ~75% plus the crucial caveat that a deeper cause like crime or a collapsed employer must be diagnosed first) and clean reversible tests. D has the most original and economically literate insight (landlords rationally hold vacancy to avoid crystallizing a loss / protect loan-covenant valuations, fixed by a city master-lease), but it is lighter on naming its own biases and offers no probability, so it lands third. C is well-reasoned with a good inversion frame and the contagious-vacancy mechanism, but its options are the most conventional, it gives no falsifiers, and it is the least explicit on confidence, so it ranks last.
